focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 15

He was three steps past Barnabas the Barmy before he understood what had just happened.

On the way to breakfast, he'd noticed that the Permanent Sticking Charm on the portrait of Barnabas the Barmy was failing along its lower-left edge.

Not the portrait itself, not the frame. The agreement beneath the charm — the substrate commitment someone had made centuries ago, probably a long-dead Hogwarts custodian who'd wanted the painting to stay put — was thinning where it anchored to the stone. Old mortar crumbling between bricks, the structure sound but the joints going. A decade more and Barnabas would start to swing.

He kept walking.

He hadn't translated. He hadn't seen the portrait and then reached back through the notation he'd memorized, matching the visible effect to a substrate grammar, working out what was there the way he'd been doing for months — slow, effortful, one concept at a time, like reading a foreign language with a dictionary open beside the text. He'd just seen it. The agreement, the thinning, the timescale. All of it at once, the way you saw a word in English and knew what it meant without sounding out the letters.

He stopped in the corridor. A seventh-year Ravenclaw carrying an armful of textbooks so tall she had to tilt her chin to see over them edged past with a muttered "excuse me," and Gavin stepped aside without looking at her. His hands were shaking — the left one worse than the right, as it always was since the Cruciatus — but the shaking wasn't from fear. Something in his head had settled into place the way a dislocated joint settles back, and the relief was physical and strange and slightly sickening.

By the time he reached the Great Hall, he suspected something in his head had shifted. The enchanted ceiling made him sure. It...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...rewriting the bottom half of the rota, moving names around to cover the slot he'd left open.

In the dormitory his bed curtains were still half-open from the night before. He stripped off the wet robes and pulled on a dry shirt, a jumper, and a fresh robe with one cuff coming unstitched where he'd caught it on a trunk latch last week. His fingers fumbled the buttons twice before they worked. On the bed lay his Charms book, the unfinished Potions essay, and the Herbology growth log with three days of blank boxes in the middle like teeth knocked out.

He looked at the lot of it and, for one clean second, saw the day the way everyone else would have seen it: missed work, annoyed teachers, Miren downstairs doing his half hour because he wouldn't, Duncan trying to make things easier by pretending he could. They were ordinary failures, the kind that had names.

Then the other scale came back and crowded it out.

He put the Potions essay under the bed. He slid the Herbology log into the bottom drawer of his trunk without filling the missing boxes. He took the Sprout note out of his pocket, folded it smaller, and tucked it into his Charms textbook so he wouldn't forget where to be after lunch. Then he added his wand and the thin notebook where he'd been copying maintenance passages in pencil from the manuscript.

That was the day's shape. It was enough school to stay in motion and enough apology to stop people asking for the wrong sort of explanation. Every other usable piece would go somewhere under the castle.

When he came back through the common room, Miren had tied the supply bag shut and slung it over her shoulder. The rota on the table now had her name in two places. She did not look up. Gavin took that, put the Sprout note deeper into the Charms book, and went to class.
